ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT VIDEO TECH SERVICES, INC'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND:

Defendant Video Tech Services' Motion to Set Aside Default Pursuant to the mandatory relief provisions of Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b) is granted.

Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b) mandates that the court vacate a default where the moving party's attorney admits in a sworn declaration that counsel's mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or neglect resulted in the default being entered. The court must grant relief even if the neglect was inexcusable, unless the court finds that the attorney's mistake, inadvertence, surprise or neglect did not in fact cause the default. (Metropolitan Service Corp. v. Casa de Palms, Ltd. (1995) 31 Cal.App.4th 1481, 1487.) The attorney's fault need not be the exclusive cause of default, so long as it was in fact a proximate cause. (Milton v. Perceptual Development Corp. (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 861, 867.) Defendant provides a sworn attorney affidavit of fault in which Mr. Kosnett attests to his mistake at the time default was entered. (Kosnett Decl. 3 ["I mistakenly believed, due to miscommunication with Defendant Video Tech Services, Inc., that Defendant did not wish to defend in the instant matter."].) Mr. Kosnett declares that default was entered because of his error "in not timely filing a responsive pleading," and he accepts "fault for the default." (Kosnett Decl. 5-6.) Therefore, the court finds that Defendant is entitled to relief.

Plaintiff attempted to file the motion within the requisite six-month time period (albeit on the very last day of that period). Relief is therefore mandatory under Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b).

Finally, Plaintiff is entitled to reasonable fees incurred in obtaining the default pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b). (See Vanderkous v. Conley (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 111, 118-119.) However, Plaintiff's request for the fees and costs he incurred in opposing this motion is groundless. (See Rogalski v. Nabers Cadillac (1992) 11 Cal.App.4th 816, 823; LeClerc Decl., 16.) Further, Plaintiff's contention that it required over $5,000 to prepare, file, and serve a simple request for entry of default is unreasonable. Defendant's counsel shall pay $1,000 as reasonable attorney's fees and costs to Plaintiff for obtaining the default.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling via email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
